summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on Steve McConnell's pyramid-building analogy for software projects, where daily progress must either move you closer to completion or reduce the remaining work required. He contrasts brute-force approaches that create an illusion of progress with smart planning that enables sustainable velocity. The post connects this to Joel Spolsky's 'Fire and Motion' principle—the idea that consistent forward movement, however imperfect, is what ultimately delivers results. Atwood concludes that good software comes not from genius or death marches, but from regular developers applying daily discipline and continuous improvement.
key_points: Progress that feels productive may still be insufficient—moving the block 10 meters when you need 100 means falling behind despite visible effort
Smart teams invest time upfront in planning and infrastructure (the 'rollers and roads') rather than immediately applying brute force
Continuous process improvement compounds over time, like adding extra rollers or creating harnesses to distribute work
Software projects are vulnerable to 'last-minute syndrome' because progress is intangible and urgency feels distant at the start
The key daily question for any project: Did we move one day closer, or reduce remaining work by one day?
money_quotes: Every day, a software project manager should ask, 'Did we move the block one day closer to our destination today? If not, did we reduce our remaining work by one day?'
Good software isn't magically conjured by superhuman geniuses, or cruelly constructed in endless, sadistic death marches by coding slaves.
Good software is built by regular developers using a daily regimen of hard work and careful planning.

You have to move forward a little bit, every day. It doesn't matter if your code is lame and buggy and nobody wants it. If you are moving forward, writing code and fixing bugs constantly, time is on your side.
Just spend a few hours every day moving the next stone block more efficiently than you did the last one, and it'll happen.
key_insight: Sustainable software progress requires daily forward movement combined with continuous investment in working more efficiently—not heroic sprints or brute-force effort.
